items.rationale: Treated as the crux of why crypto matters and the attribute that changes asset risk profiles; the house organizes both investing and policy advocacy around protecting it.

citations.quote: Decentralized verification is the hardest problem and crux of why crypto matters
Decentralization is the key to unlocking web3's potential and navigating regulatory uncertainty. But it's hard.

items.rationale: Viewed as the most powerful cryptographic tech of the decade, enabling privacy, scaling and verifiable computation; new lookup-based designs seen as a paradigm shift toward simpler, auditable SNARKs.

citations.quote: Introducing Lasso and Jolt, two new works that I hope will change how SNARKs are designed and built.
the implications of zero knowledge proofs being a thing may be as dramatic as the implications of smart contracts

items.rationale: Seen as a prerequisite for mainstream adoption; all-transparent chains compromise users, and programmable privacy (e.g., Aztec) unlocks broader DeFi, identity, gaming use.

citations.quote: It's hard to imagine crypto ever reaching mainstream adoption without privacy.
Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption.

items.rationale: L2s prized less for raw performance than for unlocking permissionless architectural innovation atop Ethereum; danksharding/DAS seen as the elegant path to cheap full scaling.

citations.quote: The most important benefit of L2s is not performance. It is that it unlocks permissionless innovation on top of Ethereum.
the scaling story for Ethereum is rollups

items.rationale: Tokens framed as 'value vectors' that preserve high-dimensionality of value, align incentives, distribute risk/reward and preserve decentralization — far beyond fundraising or equity analogues.

citations.quote: Tokens aren't equity. The design space is so much bigger.
Tokens are value vectors. Dollars are value scalars

items.rationale: Novel community-owned IP creation called a surprising breakout app; on-chain remix/attribution graphs make composition and downstream compensation natural ('multiplayer for IP').

citations.quote: Recursive, networked creativity. Multiplayer for IP. Made possible with crypto.
novel IP creation... has been in my view like one of the most... successful... applications to date

items.rationale: Crypto can crowdsource and incentivize idle compute, data and models, countering big-tech compute advantage; many high-value crypto/AI app ideas are seen as compute-constrained today.

citations.quote: The future of machine learning is decentralized
90% of the unfeasible but good ideas I hear are primarily limited by compute.

items.rationale: Belief that energy, transport and wireless networks should be decentralized; viability gated by whether verification cost is below incumbent margin.

citations.quote: We should have a decentralized energy grid, transportation network, and wireless network
Verification cost < incumbent margin... big if true

items.rationale: Web2 social punishes builders and users via centralization; open, user-owned protocols with portable follow-graphs and messaging seen as the fix, though naive p2p designs risk re-centralizing.

citations.quote: This shows why we need social media based on open protocols
Farcaster is the neutral platform we wanted all along.

items.rationale: Fully on-chain game contracts act as building blocks anyone can extend/mod; players own assets and the worlds they build, a structurally new design paradigm.

citations.quote: building a future with composability, interoperability, and permanence at the core of gameplay
I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years.

items.rationale: DAOs seen as fundamental to web3 but legally exposed; UNAs and new state statutes (CA, TX) provide legal existence, liability protection and anonymity while keeping operations decentralized.

citations.quote: This new California DAO bill could change all of that.
the UNA is now confirmed as the only viable DAO entity structure that enables the preservation of anonymity.

items.rationale: Merge-staking lets new middleware borrow Ethereum's economic security, transforming systems from capital-limited to operations-limited and reducing systemic risk by pooling provisioning.

citations.quote: eigencloud is another great way to extend Ethereum's capabilities without fragmenting network effects.
merge taking completely transforms this economics... the dominant cost is... the capital cost of staking

items.rationale: Seen as the dominant existential risk: deliberate uncertainty used as leverage, perceived endgame of banning the tech, no guidance since 2019, harming good actors and driving development offshore.

citations.quote: the SEC's endgame for crypto/web3 in the United States is about one thing: banning the technology.
Chairman Gensler has continued to demonstrate that he only knows how to regulate through sporadic enforcement cases.

items.rationale: Repeated collapses (FTX, Celsius, Voyager, Genesis) framed as failures of centralized trust, not the technology; custody is a liability and trust must be regulated unlike trustless systems.

citations.quote: Shoutout to the CeFi players out there demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless.
The risks of CeFi and DeFi are different

items.rationale: Considered a real, persistent tax on users; bad MEV erodes welfare and breaks mechanism design (EIP-1559 no longer incentive-compatible with MEV), motivating fair-ordering and OFA research.

citations.quote: Mev remains what I would consider a problem for users users are losing a lot of money to arbitragers
with MEV, *no* non-trivial or approximately welfare-maximizing TFM can be both DSIC and BPIC!

items.rationale: Bad collateral (not algorithms per se) blamed for Terra-style death spirals; uncollateralized designs dismissed as upside-less, with collateralization the key risk lever.
items.age_note: 2022 framing 'algos bad' refined to 'collateralization matters'; held into 2023

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside.
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins.'

items.rationale: Smart-contract dev seen as the opposite of agile — costly and still insecure; ZK circuit bugs are uniquely hard to scrutinize because only the proof, not the malicious input, is visible.

citations.quote: smart contract development is far from easy and far from secure at this stage unfortunately still
Security bugs in ZK circuits will be challenging.

items.rationale: Running FRI non-interactively at 80 bits of security is seen as feasibly attackable and a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem securing hundreds of millions in assets.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible... a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem.
if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology

items.rationale: A single liquid-staking entity controlling over a third of staked ETH raises consensus-centralization concern, mitigated only by client-configurable safety thresholds.

citations.quote: 8 million out of 22.7 million e... Staked are currently with Lido... controlling basically more than a third of the stake

items.rationale: One-size-fits-all licensing applied to game studios and decentralized systems (e.g., pause functions) seen as punishing ownership and decentralization, pushing devs to block Californians.

citations.quote: AB 39... could be a disaster for the video game industry.
The bill's 'Control' definition threatens decentralization

items.rationale: Rollups fragment liquidity/interoperability, and a shared sequencer with ordering monopoly could extract the most rent and trend toward winner-take-all centralization.

citations.quote: this shared sequencer proposer is the entity... that has Monopoly on The Ordering... able to extract the most rent

items.rationale: Quantum attackers could forge signatures, fork PoS and advantage PoW miners; post-quantum signatures are far larger/slower — a ~15-year horizon worth watching now.

citations.quote: if somebody builds a quantum computer today the internet is gone
the security model of bitcoin needs to change if it's going to last the next few decades.

items.rationale: Treated as the clear category leader with dominant developer mindshare, a rich composable ecosystem, and post-Merge PoS security; staking now secures a ~$400B on-chain economy.

citations.quote: Other layer 1 blockchains have grown rapidly... but @ethereum is the clear leader. Its overwhelming mindshare among web3 devs
On Ethereum, it's used to secure a $400 billion economy of ETH, ERC20 tokens, and NFTs.

items.rationale: Seen as technically a better gold than gold via enforced scarcity; excitement about restoring programmability via a SNARK-verify opcode, tempered by long-term fee-only security concerns.
items.age_note: Long-held store-of-value view; 2023 interest in ZK/programmability

citations.quote: from a purely technical point of view Bitcoin is just evidently a better version of gold than gold is
adding a VERIFY_SNARK opcode to the bitcoin protocol — would make bitcoin totally programmable

items.rationale: Reserve quality and transparency repeatedly flagged; during stress markets fear its ability to maintain the peg, yet it ironically gains share as a US-regulatory-arbitrage haven, concentrating risk.

citations.quote: there were a lot of concerns about the quality of the assets of tether and which also was particularly not transparent

items.rationale: Held up as the canonical failure of endogenous collateral and extreme censorship-resistance maximalism; cited as the lesson that pragmatism and exogenous collateral are required.
items.age_note: Collapsed May 2022; cited as historical cautionary tale, not a live position

citations.quote: The difference between endogenous and exogenous collateral is pretty significant.
recent collapses like Terra

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally bullish and actively deploying through the bear market into infra and app-layer bets (Gensyn, Proof of Play, Story Protocol, Sound, LayerZero) on a 'still very early / stay the course' thesis — while tactically cautious post-FTX and treating US regulatory hostility, not technology risk, as the dominant near-term threat. Net: long-horizon conviction high, near-term posture guarded. Grounded in repeated 2023 'keep building' messaging: 2023-03-24 'Stay The Course... Those who build... will ultimately triumph'; 2023-06-11 'still in the early innings'.

notes: Corpus spans May 2022–Oct 2023; recency-weighted toward 2023. The house 'consensus' is really a blend of distinct voices — Miles Jennings (legal/decentralization/policy), Chris Dixon & Eddy Lazzarin (thesis/Ethereum/infra), Scott Kominers & Tim Roughgarden (market design/NFTs/MEV), Dan Boneh & Justin Thaler (ZK/SNARK security), Ali Yahya/Arianna Simpson (investments) — but they cohere around one organizing belief: decentralization is the core value and the attribute that changes legal/risk profiles. The single loudest signal is concern, not excitement: SEC regulation-by-enforcement dominates 2022–23 commentary and colors everything. Most excitement is structural (multi-year theses) rather than tactical trades; the team rarely takes explicit token-price positions, favoring sector/infrastructure conviction, so tokens_excited/concerned are sparse and inferred from qualitative emphasis. Stablecoin stance is nuanced and evolved: from 'algorithmic = bad' (mid-2022) to 'collateralization is what matters' (held through 2023). Several technical concerns (ZK circuit bugs, low SNARK security parameters, PoS/liquid-staking concentration, shared-sequencer centralization, quantum) come from research-side voices and are real but lower-conviction relative to the regulatory and CeFi concerns.
